explanation.text: The mother searches for wandering turkeys even after Dad says they will return, and she says she takes care of whatever she has. Her principle and actions reveal that she feels responsible for what belongs to the family.
A sulky person appears dark, unhappy, or moody. Describing the cold-season sky as "sulky and dark" therefore suggests a gloomy appearance rather than snowiness or unpredictability.

The narrator delights in the announcer's formal words and turns "spring wheat" into an image of frozen ground, raw air, and dark skies. This imaginative response reveals both her love of language and her vivid imagination.

The family must sell its wheat immediately at a sufficient price to pay for the narrator's university plans. This financial stake is essential to a summary because it explains why the broadcast and price increase matter so much.
The elevator rises visibly from the crossroads and, with the railroad station, marks Gotham as a town. Comparing it to a monument suggests that it is a prominent, recognizable landmark.

Dad explains that the war limited his college education to one year, then promises that nothing will do the same to Ellen. In this context, "spoiled" means the war took away his opportunity to continue college.

The first-person narration gives direct access to Ellen's reflections on the harvest, the broadcast's language, her parents, and her hopes for college. This develops her point of view because readers learn how she interprets events through her private thoughts.

The children "clamored" around Baum and followed in his wake whenever he walked through town. Those energetic words indicate that they eagerly sought him out because they admired him and his fantasy stories.

The author contrasts disguised lectures with Baum's colorful fantasies in which everyday objects come alive. The stories seem more entertaining than educational because their purpose is imaginative delight rather than delivering a lesson.

Paragraph 3 centers on The Wonderful Wizard of Oz, Baum's thirteen later Oz books, their many characters, and the series' lasting appeal. It develops the central idea because those details describe the work for which Baum became best known.

Because illness made Baum a sedentary and solitary child, he read fairy tales constantly while being educated at home. His later career creating fantasy worlds suggests that this early reading strongly influenced his writing.

The "winding" road announces a pattern of changing jobs, setbacks, moves, failed businesses, and eventual success as an author. It functions as a topic sentence because the remaining paragraphs trace that indirect route.

Baum faces childhood illness, uncertain acting work, lost family money, business failures, and years of professional dead ends before succeeding. Because obstacles recur throughout the biography, overcoming many difficulties is a central idea rather than one isolated fact.

Prairie children use Baum's fantasies to escape lives of bleakness and hard work, while the broke Baum returns to fantasy after his store fails. For both creator and audience, the stories provide an entertaining refuge from harsh real-life conditions.

Eliza experiences each letter as glowing color, shape, movement, warmth, or touch while watching it transform into the next letter. This elaborate inner imagery reveals that she has a powerful imagination.

Eliza remembers expectant silence, the feeling of stopping time, and an endless walk to the microphone as parts of a nightmare. The tone becomes tense because these anxious images make the spelling bee feel intensely stressful to her.

Eliza's desire to win a difficult word, prove her father wrong, and trust the guiding letters establishes the conflict that drives the scene. The quotation therefore expresses the central idea of her struggle to turn her unusual inner process into success.

Eliza wonders how the judge sees her, struggles to clear distracting images from her mind, and must force the first letter to appear. These details reveal her difficulty using her method to spell the assigned word correctly.

Eliza interprets the long silence as proof that she is correct and already imagines the famous host and trophy as hers. Her certainty that she has succeeded reveals confidence just before the bell proves her wrong.

Eliza's unusual method of emptying her mind and seeing letters transform is central to both her earlier success and her final spelling attempt. It belongs in a summary because it drives the main action and conflict, unlike details about the round or word definition.

The narration stays close to Eliza as she watches contestants freeze, struggle, succeed, or leave and then anticipates her own turn. Her descriptions of the competition build suspense because every performance raises the stakes for what she must do next.
